name: Visibility Meter
description: A player-facing indicator that communicates how visible the player is to observers.
design_notes: A visibility meter translates lighting, stance, cover, distance, movement, and enemy perception into readable feedback. It should inform without revealing more than the game wants the player to know.
design_purpose: Connect lighting, stance, and movement to AI detection.
Reduce confusion when detection rules are systemic.
player_fantasy: The player feels able to read shadows and exposure like a skilled infiltrator.

parameters.name: visibility_sample_points
parameters.description: Body points sampled for exposure calculations.

parameters.name: light_exposure_weight
parameters.description: Weight assigned to lighting in the meter score.

parameters.name: movement_exposure_multiplier
parameters.description: Multiplier applied while moving quickly or loudly.

parameters.name: meter_smoothing_ms
parameters.description: Smoothing window before UI changes reach the displayed score.

edge_cases: Multiple enemies can perceive different visibility scores from different angles.
Flashing lights or moving shadows can create noisy meter updates.
Crouch, prone, and cover states must agree with AI perception.
common_bugs: UI meter uses lighting only while AI detects by line of sight and distance.
Smoothing hides sudden exposure and makes detection feel unfair.
Meter remains hidden during tutorials where players need it most.
balancing_notes: Precise meters reduce uncertainty; vague indicators preserve tension.
Visibility should be useful but not so exact that stealth becomes spreadsheet play.
accessibility_notes: Avoid color-only hidden/detected states and allow meter scaling or alternate icon modes.

implementation_notes.unity: Sample lighting or authored stealth volumes and feed the result into the same perception score used by AI where possible.
implementation_notes.godot: Combine Area-based shadow volumes, ray checks, and movement state into a visibility score updated on a fixed cadence.
implementation_notes.unreal: Use perception components, lighting volumes, or gameplay tags to compute exposure and replicate only needed UI state.
implementation_notes.web: Compute exposure from tile, light, and cover data in the simulation model, then render the meter as a separate UI binding.
